title: Conjecture 3 (Akbari et al.)
statement_text: Every graph $G$ with minimum degree at least $2$ has an induced linear forest with at least $\sum_{v\in V(G)}\frac{2}{d(v)+1}$ vertices.
context_text: Akbari, Amanihamedani, Mousavi, Nikpey, and Sheybani conjectured in 2019 that the Alon–Kahn–Seymour bound for induced forests (the $k=1$ case) can be achieved by a linear forest—one in which every component is a path—provided the graph has minimum degree at least 2. The minimum-degree condition is necessary, as shown by the claw $K_{1,3}$. The original authors proved the conjecture for regular graphs as supporting evidence.
attributed_to: Akbari, Amanihamedani, Mousavi, Nikpey, and Sheybani
paper_contribution: Proves Conjecture 3 in full generality as a corollary of Theorem 4, which shows every graph $G$ (with no minimum-degree restriction) has an induced linear forest with at least $f(G)$ vertices where $f(d)=1$ for $d=0$, $f(d)=5/6$ for $d=1$, and $f(d)=2/(d+1)$ for $d\geqslant 2$.
paper_title: A Caro-Wei bound for induced linear forests in graphs
